---
title: "The pathways"
editor: visual
---

# Pathway 1: The College Completer Pathway

## **What This Pathway Means**

The college completer pathway describes Southwest Minnesota high school graduates who go on to earn a postsecondary credential — whether a sub- baccalaureate certificate or associate degree, or a bachelor's degree or higher. Among the roughly 50,830 graduates in the full study population from the 2008 through 2018 cohorts, approximately 53% ultimately complete some form of postsecondary credential. This is the pathway most visible in public discourse about education and workforce development — it is the pathway that high school academic preparation is explicitly designed to support, and it is the pathway around which most regional postsecondary infrastructure is organized.

But the college completer pathway is not a single destination. It is better understood as a funnel with two exits: one that returns graduates to the regional economy and one that routes them away from it, often permanently. Which exit a graduate takes is shaped by a sequential sorting process that begins in high school, is mediated by where and what credentials are earned, and is largely determined before graduation. Understanding the college completer pathway is therefore not simply a question of who completes postsecondary education — it is a question of where they complete it and what that means for the regional economy that produced them.

## **What the Analysis Told Us**

**Postsecondary Graduation Location Is the Dominant Variable**

The single most important finding for the college completer pathway is that where a graduate earns their credential matters more than what credential they earn. This finding emerges with unusual clarity from the full population multivariate confirmation analysis, where the combined postsecondary pathway variable (ps.pathway) — capturing both credential level and graduation location simultaneously — produced the second largest improvement in model fit of any block entry (ΔAIC=-605.85) and contained the strongest individual predictor in the fully specified model.

Graduates who earn a sub-baccalaureate credential from a regional institution show the highest odds of sustained regional employment of any postsecondary pathway category (OR=2.086, 95% CI \[1.913, 2.273\], p\<0.001) relative to those who did not graduate from postsecondary at all. Graduates who earn a bachelor's degree or higher from a regional institution show similarly strong odds (OR=1.914, 95% CI \[1.711, 2.140\], p\<0.001). Both regional graduation categories show odds ratios of approximately 2.0 — meaning graduates who complete their credential at a regional institution are roughly twice as likely to be in sustained regional employment at five years post-graduation as non-completers, regardless of credential level.

The picture reverses sharply for non-regional graduation. Graduates who earn a bachelor's degree or higher from a non-regional Minnesota institution show significantly lower odds of regional employment than even non-completers (OR=0.652, 95% CI \[0.586, 0.725\], p\<0.001). Graduates who earn a bachelor's degree or higher from an institution outside Minnesota also show lower regional employment odds than non-completers (OR=0.777, 95% CI \[0.698, 0.863\], p\<0.001). Completing a sub-baccalaureate credential from a non-regional Minnesota institution shows modestly higher regional employment odds than non-completion (OR=1.385), while completing a sub-baccalaureate credential outside Minnesota shows no significant advantage over non-completion (OR=1.043, p=0.522).

The practical implication of these findings is stark: a Southwest Minnesota graduate who earns a bachelor's degree from the University of Minnesota Twin Cities is less likely to be working in the region at year 5 than a graduate who never attended postsecondary at all. Regional graduation is the hinge on which the college completer pathway turns.

**What Predicts Whether Someone Completes Postsecondary**

The full population multivariate analysis of postsecondary graduation (ps.grad) reveals which variables are most strongly associated with completing a credential. The demographic baseline model for postsecondary graduation explains a substantially larger share of variance (Nagelkerke R²=0.195) than the demographic baseline for workforce participation (R²=0.006), confirming that demographic characteristics are far more predictive of credential completion than of where graduates ultimately work.

Economic status is the single largest barrier to postsecondary completion, with economically disadvantaged graduates showing odds of graduation less than 40% those of their non-disadvantaged peers (OR=0.360, p\<0.001). This is the largest effect size observed for any binary predictor in the postsecondary graduation baseline model.

Special education status shows an equally dramatic barrier effect (OR=0.275, p\<0.001) — special education graduates face odds of postsecondary graduation less than 30% those of non-special education graduates, the lowest odds ratio observed for any predictor in the baseline model.

Gender shows a strong positive association with postsecondary graduation, with females showing substantially higher odds than males (OR=1.679, p\<0.001) — one of the most robust findings across the entire analysis and consistent with national patterns of female postsecondary attainment advantage.

Race/ethnicity produces the most severe disparities in postsecondary completion. American Indian graduates show odds of graduation less than one third those of White graduates (OR=0.314, p\<0.001). Hispanic graduates show odds less than half those of White graduates (OR=0.463, p\<0.001). Black graduates (OR=0.788, p=0.005) and Asian/PI graduates (OR=0.833, p=0.011) also show significantly lower completion odds. These disparities are among the most consequential equity findings in the full project — they represent structural barriers to credential attainment that persist after controlling for economic status, special education status, and English learner status.

**What Predicts Regional vs. Non-Regional Graduation**

Among postsecondary completers, the multivariate analysis of graduation location reveals a distinct and analytically important set of predictors. Academic orientation variables that increase the likelihood of postsecondary completion are also the variables most strongly associated with graduating from non-regional institutions.

AP exam participation is the strongest predictor of non-regional graduation among high school accomplishment variables. ACT participation, higher MCA Reading scores, and PSEO participation all independently predict graduation from non-regional institutions. The preparation that most reliably gets students into and through postsecondary education is precisely the preparation that routes them away from regional institutions.

CTE engagement shows the opposite pattern. Higher CTE achievement and greater CTE course volume are associated with graduation from regional institutions when postsecondary is completed at all — confirming that CTE engagement operates as a regional anchoring mechanism even within the college completer pathway.

EDR matters here in a specific and important way. EDR 6E Southwest Central graduates, who show the strongest academic orientation indicators and the largest shares of four-year college attendance, also show the highest rates of non-regional graduation. EDR 6W and EDR 8 graduates show modestly higher odds of regional postsecondary graduation after demographic adjustment, consistent with stronger regional institutional ties and fewer out-of-region postsecondary alternatives drawing graduates away.

**The Academic Preparation Paradox**

The most important and most uncomfortable finding within the college completer pathway is what might be called the academic preparation paradox. The variables that most strongly predict postsecondary completion — high ACT scores, AP exam participation, strong MCA Reading performance, PSEO participation — are simultaneously the variables most strongly associated with non-regional graduation and non- regional employment. The full population multivariate analysis confirms this at every stage: in the workforce participation models, ACT participation (OR=0.839), AP exam participation (OR=0.645), and MCA Reading score (OR=0.883) are all significantly and independently associated with lower odds of regional employment even after controlling for postsecondary pathway. In the graduation location models, these same variables predict graduation from non-regional institutions.

This means the region is caught in a structural bind that the data make quantitatively precise. Investments in academic preparation increase the probability that students will complete postsecondary credentials — which is unambiguously good for those students. But those same investments, to the extent they route students toward non- regional institutions, also decrease the probability that those credentials will return to the regional economy. The region develops human capital that it then loses, and the better the academic preparation, the more likely the loss.

**What Is Not Meaningful Within This Pathway**

Several variables that might be expected to matter within the college completer pathway show limited or no independent explanatory power in the multivariate models.

Average county wages as a percent of the state average shows negligible practical significance throughout — while statistically significant in some specifications, the absolute magnitude of wage differences across groups is too small to constitute a meaningful labor market pull or push factor.

English learner status, while significantly associated with lower postsecondary graduation odds (OR=0.838 at baseline), does not show significant independent associations with graduation location or regional employment after other variables are controlled — suggesting its primary effect within this pathway operates through reduced credential completion rather than geographic sorting among completers.

Non-English home language shows substantial overlap with English learner status and adds no independent explanatory power beyond it in multivariate specifications.

## **The Bottom Line for the College Completer Pathway**

The college completer pathway is the most consequential for regional workforce development precisely because it is the most powerful driver of credential attainment — and the most reliable exporter of the credentials it produces. The data identify regional postsecondary graduation as the critical hinge variable: graduates who complete credentials at regional institutions return to the regional workforce at roughly twice the rate of non-completers, while graduates who complete at non-regional institutions are less likely to be in regional employment than graduates who never attended postsecondary at all.

The variables that define success within this pathway — high academic achievement, AP participation, ACT preparation — are the same variables that route graduates away from regional institutions. The variables that partially counteract this departure dynamic — CTE engagement, regional postsecondary attendance, sub-baccalaureate credential completion — are currently undersupported relative to their demonstrated regional retention value. And the demographic barriers that most severely limit access to this pathway — economic disadvantage, special education status, American Indian and Hispanic race/ethnicity — represent structural equity challenges that educational programming alone cannot fully address but that dramatically constrain who the college completer pathway is available to in the first place.

<br>

# Pathway 2: The Workforce-First Pathway

## **What This Pathway Means**

The workforce-first pathway describes Southwest Minnesota high school graduates who enter the regional labor market directly after high school without completing a postsecondary credential — and who successfully achieve sustained regional employment. Among the roughly 47% of the study population who never complete a postsecondary credential, this pathway represents the most desirable outcome from a regional workforce retention perspective. These are the graduates who stay, who work, and who anchor themselves to the regional economy — often in the manufacturing, agricultural, construction, and skilled trades sectors that form the backbone of Southwest Minnesota's economic base.

The workforce-first pathway is not simply the absence of postsecondary completion. It is a distinct and identifiable trajectory shaped by specific high school experiences, principally CTE engagement, that produces durable regional employment attachment across a decade of observation. It is the pathway the regional economy most depends on, and it is the pathway that the data most consistently and powerfully illuminate.

Understanding this pathway requires setting aside the conventional assumption that postsecondary completion is the only legitimate successful outcome for high school graduates. Among non-college completers in Southwest Minnesota, approximately 25-27% achieve sustained regional employment at years 5 and 10 — a share that has remained remarkably stable across the observation window and that represents a substantial and economically vital regional workforce contribution. The question the analysis answers is not whether this pathway is viable but what determines who follows it successfully and who does not.

## **What the Analysis Told Us**

**CTE Course-Taking Is the Dominant and Defining Variable**

No finding in the entire project is as consistent, as precise, or as temporally stable as the association between total CTE courses taken and sustained regional employment among non-college completers. Across three time points spanning a decade — year 1, year 5, and year 10 — and across all three model specifications at each time point, total CTE courses taken produces an odds ratio of approximately 1.055 for sustained regional employment. The estimates are OR=1.057 at year 1, OR=1.054 at year 5, and OR=1.056 at year 10 — a range so narrow across a decade of observation that it constitutes one of the most robust empirical findings in the project.

The practical meaning of this association is a clear dose-response relationship: each additional CTE course taken in high school is associated with approximately a 5.5% increase in the odds of sustained regional employment, regardless of when that employment is measured. A graduate who took six CTE courses has meaningfully higher regional employment odds than a graduate who took three, who in turn has meaningfully higher odds than a graduate who took one. The relationship is linear, persistent, and not explained away by any other variable in the model.

CTE achievement level — the categorical measure of CTE engagement — confirms the dose-response pattern from a different analytical angle. By year 5, CTE concentrators show sustained regional employment rates of 30.9% compared to 26.3% for CTE participants and just 16.9% for graduates with no CTE engagement — a gap of 14 percentage points between the highest and lowest CTE engagement categories. The no MN employment record rate tells the complementary story: 29.9% for concentrators, 33.4% for participants, and 47.6% for no-CTE graduates by year 5. Nearly half of all graduates with no CTE engagement are absent from Minnesota employment records five years after high school graduation.

In the multivariate confirmation analysis, CTE concentrators or completors show the largest positive association of any high school accomplishment variable in the full population model — OR=1.572 (95% CI \[1.459, 1.693\], p\<0.001) relative to no-CTE graduates, with a clear dose- response confirmed by CTE participants (OR=1.324). These associations persist through the addition of postsecondary pathway controls in Block 4, confirming that CTE engagement has independent effects on regional workforce retention beyond its influence on postsecondary pathway selection.

**Academic Orientation Works Against This Pathway**

Within the workforce-first pathway, academic orientation variables operate as consistent negative predictors of regional employment — not because academic achievement is harmful, but because it signals and reinforces an orientation toward non-regional destinations. Among non- college completers specifically, ACT participation shows odds ratios of OR=0.847 at year 1, OR=0.817 at year 5, and OR=0.826 at year 10 — all significant, all in the same direction, and all surviving full multivariate controls. MCA Reading proficiency shows an even cleaner progressive pattern: OR=0.935 at year 1, OR=0.904 at year 5, and OR=0.867 at year 10 — strengthening with every passing year as higher-achieving non- completers progressively sort away from regional employment over time.

These findings do not suggest that academic achievement should be discouraged. They document a real sorting mechanism: graduates who developed stronger academic skills and college preparedness behaviors are less likely to remain in regional employment — even when they ultimately do not complete a credential. The academic orientation departure mechanism operates independently of credential completion status.

**Gender Shapes Who Follows This Pathway**

Gender is a consistent and significant predictor of workforce-first pathway success across all three time points and all model specifications. Female non-college completers show odds of sustained regional employment of OR=0.836 at year 1, OR=0.795 at year 5, and OR=0.826 at year 10 relative to males — associations that survive the full set of academic orientation and CTE engagement controls. Males are substantially more likely to achieve sustained regional employment through this pathway, and the gap is not fully explained by differential CTE engagement or academic orientation.

The bivariate evidence reinforces this finding dramatically. By year 10, 27.4% of male non- college completers achieve sustained regional employment compared to 21.1% of females — a gap of 6.3 percentage points. Meanwhile, 42.5% of female non-college completers carry no MN employment record at year 10 compared to 35.7% of males. The gender gap in regional employment widens between years 1 and 5 and stabilizes thereafter, suggesting that the differential operates through early career sorting rather than late career divergence.

What drives the residual gender effect after controlling for CTE engagement and academic orientation is not fully resolved by these data. It likely reflects a combination of differential access to and engagement with the specific CTE program areas most strongly linked to regional employer demand — manufacturing, construction, transportation, agriculture — which are disproportionately populated by male students in Southwest Minnesota high schools.

**Local Labor Market Conditions Matter at Entry and Over the Long Run**

Average county unemployment rate is a significant negative predictor of regional employment at year 1 (OR=0.897, p\<0.001) and year 10 (OR=0.923, p=0.003) but not at year 5 (OR=0.979, p=0.215) in the non-college completer models. Each one- unit increase in average unemployment rate is associated with approximately an 8-10% reduction in the odds of regional employment at both the entry and decade horizons.

The non-linear temporal pattern of this association — significant at year 1, absent at year 5, significant again at year 10 — suggests that local labor market conditions shape the availability of regional employment opportunities at the point of entry and over the long run, but that the intermediate-term sorting process is dominated by the accomplishment and orientation variables that define the two pathways. In other words, CTE engagement can partially offset local labor market disadvantage in the medium term, but the structural constraint of persistently higher local unemployment reasserts itself over a decade.

EDR shows no independent association with workforce-first pathway success at any time point after demographic and accomplishment controls, suggesting that the subregional differences in regional employment observed in bivariate analyses are attributable to compositional differences in CTE engagement and academic orientation rather than independent geographic effects.

**Special Education Status and Economic Disadvantage**

Special education status shows a complex and temporally variable association with the workforce- first pathway. At year 1, special education graduates show significantly lower regional employment odds (OR=0.750, p\<0.001) that persist through all model specifications — a finding unique to non-college completers that does not appear in the full population analysis at the demographic baseline. This suggests that special education graduates face barriers to regional employment entry that are not explained by their CTE engagement or academic achievement profiles.

By year 5, the special education association is non-significant at baseline but reverses to negative once accomplishment controls are introduced (OR=0.917, p=0.106) — and by year 10 it becomes significantly negative (OR=0.819, p=0.007) in the fully specified model. The progressive strengthening of the special education disadvantage over time, net of CTE and academic controls, suggests that barriers to sustained regional employment for special education graduates are not simply a matter of entry timing — they reflect longer-run challenges in achieving and maintaining the sustained employment threshold that defines this pathway.

Economic status shows no significant independent association with workforce-first pathway success at any time point or model specification among non-college completers — a consistent null finding across the entire analysis that suggests economic disadvantage operates primarily through its effects on postsecondary access rather than through direct effects on regional employment attachment.

**Postsecondary Attendance Without Completion**

Among graduates who attended postsecondary without completing — the non-college completers who came closest to following the college completer pathway — the regional employment trajectory reveals something important about the boundaries between pathways. The attended.ps association with regional employment moves from large and positive at year 1 (OR=1.817) to null at year 5 (OR=1.016) to modestly negative at year 10 (OR=0.885, p=0.030).

The year 1 positive association reflects concurrent enrollment and regional work — students working in the region while attending postsecondary nearby. The null year 5 association reflects the resolution of that structural artifact. The significant negative year 10 association suggests that postsecondary attendance without completion instills a geographic orientation away from regional employment that becomes apparent only over the long run. Graduates who attempted the college completer pathway but did not complete it are modestly but significantly less likely to achieve sustained regional employment a decade later than graduates who never attempted postsecondary at all — net of all other controls. This is a finding with direct implications for how postsecondary access programs targeting non-traditional students are designed and supported.

**What Is Not Meaningful Within This Pathway**

Average county wages as a percent of the state average shows no meaningful independent association with workforce-first pathway success at any time point — consistent with the negligible practical significance of this variable throughout the analysis. Local wage levels do not independently predict regional employment among non-college completers once other variables are controlled.

English learner status shows a significant negative association at year 5 in the fully specified model (OR=0.860, p=0.047) — emerging only after accomplishment controls are introduced, suggesting EL graduates' CTE engagement partially masks an underlying regional employment disadvantage — but this association does not persist to year 10 (OR=0.939, p=0.616), indicating it reflects a medium-term rather than long-run phenomenon.

Non-English home language, PSEO participation, MCA Science, and average wages are all excluded from the modeling framework for reasons documented throughout the analysis — redundancy, sample restriction, voluntary self-selection, and negligible effect sizes respectively.

## **The Bottom Line for the Workforce-First Pathway**

The workforce-first pathway is the clearest story in the entire project. CTE course-taking anchors graduates to the regional economy with a dose-response consistency and temporal stability that no other variable approaches. The mechanism does not require credential completion, does not require favorable local labor market conditions, and does not require any particular demographic profile. It requires CTE engagement — measurable in high school, independent of every other predictor, and equally powerful a decade after graduation as it is in the first year.

The policy lever is as direct as the finding is clear: expanding access to and depth of CTE engagement among the graduates most likely to enter the workforce directly after high school is the single most evidence-supported strategy for strengthening regional workforce retention in Southwest Minnesota. The graduates who most need this lever — those without academic orientation, those from higher-unemployment communities, those in demographic groups facing regional employment barriers — are exactly the graduates for whom CTE engagement produces the largest relative return in regional employment outcomes.

<br>

# Pathway 3: The Disconnected Pathway

## **What This Pathway Means**

The disconnected pathway describes Southwest Minnesota high school graduates who neither complete a postsecondary credential nor achieve sustained regional employment — and who, in the most severe cases, disappear from formal Minnesota employment records entirely. This is not a fringe outcome. It is the modal experience among non- college completers. Among graduates who never earn a postsecondary credential, 50.4% are disconnected in four or five of their first five post-exit years. By year 5, 30.7% carry no Minnesota employment record at all. By year 10, that share reaches 38.6% — making no MN employment record the single largest category among non- college completers at the decade mark.

The disconnected pathway is not a single homogeneous experience. The annual detailed analysis reveals two distinct disconnection signatures that reflect fundamentally different underlying mechanisms and require fundamentally different policy responses. Local underutilization — characterized by elevated in-region marginal employment and reduced geographic mobility — affects graduates who are present in the Minnesota labor market but unable to achieve sustained employment thresholds. Geographic absence — characterized by elevated no MN employment record rates without corresponding in-region marginal employment — affects graduates who are not present in the formal Minnesota labor market at all, either because they have left the state or because their employment is not captured by Minnesota wage records.

Understanding the disconnected pathway requires holding both signatures simultaneously, because the variables associated with each are different, the populations affected are different, and the interventions implied are different.

## **What the Analysis Told Us**

**The Absence of CTE Is the Defining Characteristic**

The most powerful predictor of disconnection is the absence of CTE engagement. No-CTE graduates show no MN employment record rates of 40.0% at year 1, 42.5% at year 2, 43.9% at year 3, 46.5% at year 4, and 47.6% at year 5 — growing consistently and substantially across the five-year window. By year 5, nearly half of all graduates with no CTE engagement are absent from formal Minnesota employment records. This is the single most alarming finding in the entire project.

The contrast with CTE concentrators is stark. CTE concentrators show no MN employment record rates of 25.0% at year 1, growing to 29.9% by year 5 — a gap of approximately 18 percentage points relative to no-CTE graduates that is present from the first post-exit year and grows with every passing year. The multivariate confirmation analysis places this in full relief: even after controlling for all demographic characteristics, local labor market conditions, and academic orientation, the absence of CTE engagement is independently and strongly associated with disconnection.

The disconnected pathway is therefore most usefully understood as what happens when neither pathway — neither the college completer pathway nor the workforce-first pathway — captures a graduate. No-CTE, no-credential graduates face the worst outcomes at every time point in the analysis. They are the population falling through the gap between both pathways.

**Two Disconnection Signatures Require Different Explanations**

The local underutilization signature is associated with special education status, economic disadvantage, lower CTE engagement, and lower academic achievement. Graduates in this group are working — they have Minnesota employment records and they show elevated in- region marginal employment rates — but they cannot achieve the sustained employment threshold of 1,000 hours with a single employer in a calendar year. Special education graduates show persistently elevated in-region marginal employment across all five annual time points, as do graduates from entirely rural communities and graduates from higher-unemployment counties. Their disconnection is a problem of employment quality and stability rather than employment absence — they are in the regional economy but cannot get traction within it.

The geographic absence signature is associated with a substantially different set of characteristics. English learner graduates show no MN employment record rates growing from 36% at year 1 to 40% by year 5, with no corresponding elevation in in-region marginal employment — suggesting they are not simply underemployed locally but genuinely absent from formal Minnesota employment. Non-English home language graduates show similar patterns with the largest absolute gap at year 1. American Indian graduates show 40-47% no MN employment record rates at every annual time point regardless of model controls. Female non-college completers show growing MN record absence, reaching 42.5% by year 10. Higher-achieving non-completers — those with ACT participation, higher MCA scores, AP experience — also show elevated no MN record rates that grow over time, but for a fundamentally different reason: they are leaving Minnesota for better opportunities elsewhere, not disengaging from formal employment.

The coexistence of these two signatures within a single analytical category is the most important structural insight from the annual detailed analysis. Treating the disconnected pathway as a single phenomenon obscures the distinction between graduates who need better local employment opportunities and graduates who need fundamentally different structural supports — and conflating the two risks designing interventions that address one signature while leaving the other entirely untouched.

**American Indian Graduates and the Most Severe Disconnection**

American Indian graduates warrant specific attention within the disconnected pathway because their profile is the most extreme and the most analytically resistant to explanation in the entire project. No MN employment record rates of 40-47% at every annual time point. A year 1 odds ratio of 0.327 for regional employment in the non-college completer multivariate baseline — the lowest of any demographic group at any time point in any model specification in the analysis. A progressive attenuation of this association to non-significance by year 10 that reflects not improvement but a selective survival effect — the American Indian graduates who remain in regional employment by year 10 are a small and increasingly unrepresentative subset of the original cohort.

These associations survive all multivariate controls. They are not explained by lower CTE engagement, lower academic achievement, economic disadvantage, English learner status, or geographic location. They point to structural barriers — historical, economic, geographic, and cultural — that are not captured by any variable in this dataset and that cannot be addressed by programming interventions that operate at the high school level alone.

**Race and Ethnicity More Broadly**

The racial and ethnic disparities in the disconnected pathway are the most persistent and least fully explained findings in the entire analysis. Black graduates show significantly lower regional employment odds across all three time points in the fully specified non-college completer models, with associations that persist after controlling for every variable available — OR=0.837 at year 1, OR=0.543 at year 5, OR=0.568 at year 10. These are not small effects and they do not attenuate with time. Asian/PI graduates show similarly persistent negative associations, strengthening to OR=0.464 at year 10 — the most extreme negative race/ethnicity association in the year 10 fully specified model.

Hispanic graduates show a more complex pattern — significant negative associations at year 1 (OR=0.810) and year 10 (OR=0.804) in the fully specified model but not at year 5 — suggesting a long-run regional employment disadvantage with a medium-term window of relative convergence that the data cannot fully explain.

The persistence of these associations through all model specifications across all time points is the most important equity finding in the project. These disparities are not artifacts of differential CTE engagement, academic preparation, or local labor market conditions. They reflect structural and systemic factors that operate independently of the educational sorting mechanisms captured in this analysis, and they represent the clearest signal in the data that educational programming interventions — however well designed — are insufficient on their own to address the equity dimensions of regional workforce disconnection.

**Gender and Long-Run Absence**

Among female non-college completers, the trajectory toward geographic absence is one of the most consistent and troubling patterns in the longitudinal data. Female non-completers show growing no MN employment record rates across every annual time point and every observation window — reaching 42.5% by year 10 compared to 35.7% for males. The gender gap in no MN record grows from approximately 1 percentage point at year 1 to nearly 7 percentage points at year 10.

The multivariate analysis confirms a significant independent gender effect on regional employment odds that is not fully explained by differential academic orientation or CTE engagement. The residual gender disadvantage in regional employment — OR=0.795 to 0.853 across the three fully specified models — points to factors beyond academic and vocational preparation, potentially including caregiving responsibilities, differential access to specific regional employment sectors, and geographic mobility constraints that fall disproportionately on women in rural Southwest Minnesota communities.

**Local Underutilization and Its Predictors**

Within the local underutilization signature, the annual detailed analysis identifies a specific set of variables associated with in- region marginal employment — work that is present but below the sustained threshold. Special education graduates show persistently elevated in-region marginal employment across all five annual time points, declining only slowly as careers develop. Entirely rural graduates show the highest no MN employment record rates of any community type (42.3% by year 5) alongside elevated marginal employment — suggesting that entirely rural communities face both a local underutilization problem and a geographic absence problem simultaneously.

Lower CTE course volume is consistently associated with higher in-region marginal employment across all time points — graduates with few CTE courses are more likely to work locally below threshold rather than achieving sustained employment. Economic disadvantage shows a counterintuitive pattern in the annual data — disadvantaged graduates actually show more local labor market engagement in early years, with slightly higher in-region marginal employment rates than non- disadvantaged graduates, suggesting greater dependence on local labor markets even when those markets cannot provide sustained employment opportunities.

**What Is Not Meaningful Within This Pathway**

Average county wages show negligible practical significance for disconnection outcomes, consistent across all analytical frameworks. Local wage levels do not independently predict disconnection after controlling for the variables that do matter.

PSEO participation, AP exam participation, and MCA Science scores are excluded from the modeling framework for reasons documented throughout the analysis — sample restriction, small group size, and voluntary self-selection respectively — and their bivariate associations with disconnection are not sufficiently robust to warrant inclusion in multivariate specifications.

Non-English home language is redundant with English learner status and adds no independent explanatory power in multivariate contexts.

## **The Bottom Line for the Disconnected Pathway**

The disconnected pathway is the most urgent workforce challenge identified in this project, the most analytically complex, and the least amenable to single-lever policy solutions. Its scale — affecting the majority of non-college completers — and its persistence — growing rather than resolving over a decade — make it the defining feature of the Southwest Minnesota workforce landscape among the nearly half of graduates who do not complete postsecondary credentials.

The clearest intervention point the data identify is CTE engagement — its absence is the single variable most consistently associated with disconnection, and its presence is the single variable most consistently associated with escape from the disconnected pathway. But CTE engagement is not a sufficient response to the full scope of disconnection in this population. The local underutilization signature requires employment quality and stability interventions that go beyond educational programming. The geographic absence signature — particularly for American Indian graduates, English learners, and female non-completers — reflects structural barriers that educational interventions alone cannot address.

The racial and ethnic disparities that persist through all model specifications across all time points are the most important signal in the data about the limits of what is measurable and addressable through the educational and labor market variables available in this analysis. They point toward the need for equity- focused interventions that operate beyond the high school level — addressing economic opportunity, geographic access, structural discrimination, and historical context in ways that no configuration of CTE courses or academic preparation can substitute for.

The disconnected pathway is ultimately a story about who the educational and labor market system is not reaching — and the data are precise about who that is even when they cannot be equally precise about why.
